result:
I’ve always believed that the best writing happens when there’s no assignment attached to it. So thank you for giving me this strange, luxurious permission: just write. No brief, no agenda, no marketing funnel to optimize. Just language running loose for a thousand words like a dog off its leash.

I’m sitting in a small wooden chair that creaks every time I inhale too deeply. It’s 3:17 a.m. and the only light in the room is the soft blue-white of the laptop screen and the ridiculous orange glow of a Himalayan salt lamp I bought during a phase I now call “my brief flirtation with pseudoscience.” The lamp looks like a glowing tumor. I love it anyway. It hums with the low electric certainty of something that doesn’t know it’s ridiculous.

Outside, rain is doing that thing where it sounds like static on an old record. The city is asleep except for the occasional ambulance siren that slices through the wet dark like a scalpel. I’ve always found sirens oddly comforting. Someone, somewhere, is still trying. That’s something.

There’s a peculiar freedom in writing without purpose. Most of the time we write to persuade, to sell, to explain, to seduce, to apologize, to remember, to mourn, to brag. We weaponize language or we gift-wrap it. But right now I’m doing none of those things. I’m just moving my fingers and watching words appear, the way a child might drag a stick through wet sand and then stand back, slightly surprised that the universe allowed the mark to remain.

I keep thinking about how much of life is spent trying to become legible to other people. We edit ourselves in real time—adjusting tone, posture, vocabulary, opinions—so that we make sense inside someone else’s story. Social media accelerated this into a kind of permanent performance anxiety. Every photograph is a casting call. Every sentence is a job interview. We are all, in some way, waiting for the world to say: “Ah yes, I see you. You belong here.”

But belonging was never meant to be a performance. The moments I’ve felt most real—most *there*—were the ones no one was watching. Like the time I sat on a freezing beach in Normandy in February, alone except for some indifferent seagulls, eating a slightly smashed pain au chocolat from my coat pocket and crying for no particular reason except that the ocean was enormous and I was tiny and both facts felt suddenly, violently beautiful.

I wonder what would happen if we all gave ourselves one unsponsored hour a week. No content creation. No side hustle. No “personal brand.” Just pure, wasteful, inefficient being. I suspect the world might break open a little. Not in some grand revolutionary way, but in the small, devastating way that happens when someone looks up from their phone on the subway and really sees the old woman across from them—the one with the shopping bags and the trembling hands—and feels something complicated and unmonetizable move through their chest.

My cat just walked across the keyboard and typed “jskdlfja;slkdjf.” I’m tempted to leave it in. It feels more honest than most of what passes for human communication these days. She’s now purring on my shoulder like a small broken engine. Her breath smells faintly of tuna. I’m absurdly in love with her.

There’s a Japanese concept called *wabi-sabi* that I keep coming back to. It’s the acceptance of transience and imperfection. The beauty of the chipped bowl, the moss on the stone, the wrinkle that wasn’t there last year. We are a culture that has declared war on our own mortality and it’s making us insane. We pathologize aging, failure, grief, uncertainty. We want everything sanded down, optimized, filtered, narrated with a tidy three-act structure and an inspirational quote at the end.

But life doesn’t have a three-act structure. It has hundreds of acts, most of them boring. It has long, stupid digressions. It has plot holes you could drive a truck through. The best parts usually happen during what we would edit out if we were directing the movie of our own lives: the twenty minutes spent watching pigeons fight over a crust of bread, the way your father says “well, shoot” when he’s proud of you, the particular smell of your childhood best friend’s basement that you somehow still remember thirty years later.

I don’t know where this is going and that feels correct. Writing with a destination in mind is like going on a hike with your eyes glued to Google Maps. You miss the weird mushrooms and the way the light hits that one tree and the sudden, irrational urge to lie down in the middle of the trail and watch clouds.

So here’s what I want instead: more useless beauty. More time wasted on things that don’t scale. More love letters that will never be sent. More dancing when no one’s playing music. More sitting on the floor eating cereal at 2 a.m. while listening to the same song on repeat until it stops being a song and starts being a blood memory. More saying the slightly wrong thing because it’s true. More forgiveness for our own contradictions. More permission to be illegible, unoptimized, gloriously beside the point.

The rain has stopped. The salt lamp is still glowing like a benevolent tumor. My cat is now asleep in the exact center of my notes for a novel I’ll probably never finish. The cursor blinks at me patiently, as if to say: *You still have four hundred words left, idiot. Say something that matters.*

But maybe nothing needs to matter in the way we usually mean it. Maybe mattering is overrated. Maybe the point is simply to leave evidence that you were here. That you noticed things. That you loved stupidly and specifically. That you were sometimes afraid and sometimes astonished and sometimes both at once.

I notice that my hands are cold but the rest of me is warm. I notice that I’m older than I feel and younger than I am. I notice that the word “lonely” and the word “alone” are not the same temperature in the mouth. I notice that writing this has made me happy in a way that feels almost embarrassing, like catching yourself smiling at your own reflection in a shop window.

There are 87 words left.

I want to use them to say that I hope you also give yourself permission sometimes. To be pointless. To be slow. To be wrong. To be moved by things that would make your more sophisticated friends roll their eyes. To write badly. To love badly. To live in a way that doesn’t make for good content but makes for a good life.

The salt lamp just flickered, like it’s agreeing with me.

And now we’ve reached one thousand words, more or less. The dog is back on the leash. The assignment is complete. But the night is still young and the world is still weird and I’m still here, typing in the dark like a reasonable lunatic.

I think that’s enough.

For now.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
